results.question: Why were Co-operative Banks denied deduction under section 80P of the Act from assessment year 2007-08?
results.expected_summary: From assessment year 2007-08 onwards, deduction under Section 80P of the Act is not available to any Co-operative Bank as per section 80P(4) of the Act. However, a Primary Agricultural Credit Society
results.actual_answer:
Co-operative Banks were denied deduction under section 80P of the Act from the assessment year 2007-08 due to several key reasons:

1. **Legislative Amendment**: The amendment to section 80P of the Act, effective from 01.04.2007, explicitly stated that no deduction under this section is available to any Co-operative Bank. This legislative change was aimed at clarifying the eligibility criteria for deductions, thereby excluding Co-operative Banks from benefiting from these provisions [Section 3.2, p.54].

2. **Nature of Activities**: Co-operative Banks are engaged in commercial banking activities, which include taking deposits and providing loans to both members and non-members. This differentiates them from other Co-operative Societies that primarily serve their members. The Income Tax Department (ITD) has clarified that while Co-operative Societies operate for the benefit of their members, Co-operative Banks do not exclusively cater to their members, thus making them ineligible for the deductions under section 80P [Section 3.2, p.54; Section 3.6.3, p.65].

3. **Judicial Rulings**: Judicial interpretations have reinforced the exclusion of Co-operative Banks from the benefits of section 80P. For instance, the jurisdictional High Court of Karnataka ruled that Co-operative Banks are not eligible for deductions under section 80P(2)(d) for interest earned from them, further solidifying the stance that these entities do not meet the criteria set forth in the Act [Section 3.10.1, p.72].

These factors collectively led to the denial of deductions for Co-operative Banks under section 80P of the Act from the assessment year 2007-08 onwards.

results.question: What are the main audit recommendations regarding PAN registration and information sharing for Co-operative Societies?
results.expected_summary: Key audit recommendations included: (1) CBDT may request Central and State level registering bodies for instituting seeding of PAN in their databases and facilitate structured institutional sharing of
results.actual_answer:
The main audit recommendations regarding PAN registration and information sharing for Co-operative Societies are:

1. The CBDT may ensure that the PAN status of the assessees claiming deductions as Co-operative Societies is only AOPs. CBDT may review the PAN registration status and ensure uniformity in PAN registration to identify the assessees pertaining to Co-operative Societies/Co-operative Banks, to facilitate meaningful information from data available with ITD [Section 3.15, p.85].

2. The CBDT may consider requesting the Central and State level registering bodies and regulatory authorities governing the Co-operative Societies and Co-operative Banks for instituting the seeding of PAN in their databases and facilitate a structured and institutional sharing of information. A process may be devised to track and monitor any change in the status of the assessee [Section 2.8, p.47].

3. The CBDT may ensure that the ITD checks for the actual status of the applicant vis-a-vis its name and activity carried out while allotting PAN to Co-operative Societies. In order to enable easy identification and monitoring of exemptions availed by the assessees, ITD may consider affixing the fourth letter as ‘A’ to the PAN of Co-operative Society [Section 2.8, p.47].

4. The CBDT may reconsider devising and monitoring structured and institutional sharing of PAN registration details and any other information with the registering bodies and regulatory authorities to prevent misuse of tax provisions by ineligible assessees [Section 2.8, p.47].

5. ITR-5 may capture a list of all Members of a Co-operative Society, along with their PAN, for the previous year relevant to the Assessment Year of filing of return. Quoting of PAN may be made mandatory for deposits received above a threshold amount by Co-operative Societies [Section 4.16, p.108].

The key issues with hospital empanelment and infrastructure under PMJAY were:

1. **Non-fulfillment of Minimum Criteria** - EHCPs did not meet the minimum criteria of support systems and infrastructure, nor conformed to quality standards as prescribed under the Guidelines [04_Executive Summary, p.0].

2. **Shortage of Infrastructure and Equipment** - There was a shortage of infrastructure, equipment, and doctors in several States/UTs, with some equipment found to be non-functional [04_Executive Summary, p.0].

3. **Delay in Empanelment** - In 14 States/UTs, 2,733 hospitals were empanelled with delays ranging from more than one day to 44 months, and in six States, empanelment of 418 hospitals was under process with delays from two days to 29 months [04_Executive Summary, p.0].

4. **Non-compliance with General Criteria** - Instances of non-compliance with the general criteria for empanelment were noted, affecting the quality of services provided [05_Chapter I - > 4.2, p.41].

5. **De-empanelment Issues** - In 11 States, 241 hospitals were de-empanelled due to low performance and malpractices, indicating issues with the quality of care [05_Chapter I - > 4.10, p.49].

6. **Lack of Specialties** - Many EHCPs did not provide all available specialties, which reduced the availability of services to beneficiaries [05_Chapter I - > 4.5 Non-empanelment of all available and eligible Specialities of EHCPs by SHA, p.44].

7. **Inadequate Utilization of Claims** - Public/Government Hospitals failed to adequately utilize the reimbursed claims for improving infrastructure and services [05_Chapter I - > 5.3, p.54].

8. **Deficiencies in Support Systems** - In 12 States/UTs, EHCPs did not meet minimum criteria, with deficiencies in medical equipment, basic infrastructure, and support services [05_Chapter I - > 4.2.1, p.41]. 

These issues highlight significant challenges in the implementation and effectiveness of the PMJAY scheme.

results.question: What were the different modes of project implementation under Bharatmala Pariyojana?
results.expected_summary: Projects under Bharatmala Pariyojana Phase-I were implemented through different modes: Engineering, Procurement and Construction (EPC) mode, Hybrid Annuity Model (HAM), Build Operate Transfer (BOT) To
results.actual_answer:
The different modes of project implementation under Bharatmala Pariyojana were:

1. **Engineering, Procurement and Construction Mode (EPC)** - Project is funded through public money and user fee is collected by Central Government through implementing agency and the same is transferred to Consolidated Fund of India (CFI) [Section 1.5, p.24].

2. **Build Operate Transfer (Toll) Mode** - Project is funded by private money and user fee is collected & retained by concessionaire over the concession period [Section 1.5, p.24].

3. **Build Operate Transfer (Annuity) Mode** - Project is funded by private money, user fee is collected by Central Government through implementing agency and the same is transferred to CFI while the concessionaire receives, after commercial operation date, pre-determined semiannual annuities over the concession period [Section 1.5, p.24].

4. **Item Rate Mode** - Payment is made to the contractor based on execution of items as per bills of quantities while user fee is collected by Central Government through implementing agency [Section 1.5, p.24].

5. **Hybrid Annuity Mode (HAM)** - Civil cost of project is funded in the ratio of 40:60 through public money and private money, user fee is collected by Central Government and the same is transferred to CFI [Section 1.5, p.24].
